summary:
The Kingdom of Benin (c. 1180–1897 AD) in its early modern peak (15th–17th centuries) was one of the most sophisticated and powerful states in West Africa, governed by the Oba (king) from the walled city of Benin (in modern Nigeria), notable for its extraordinary artistic tradition (the [[event:3062|Benin Bronzes]]), its complex bureaucratic administration, its professional army, and its highly regulated participation in trade — first with inland African states and later with Portuguese and other European traders. The kingdom reached its territorial and artistic peak under Oba Ewuare the Great (r. c. 1440–1473), who is credited with founding the elaborate palace compound, reorganizing the military, and expanding Benin's territory significantly. The massive earthwork defensive system surrounding Benin City — the longest earthwork in the world at over 16,000 km of combined wall and ditch — took an estimated 150 million man-hours to construct.

When Portuguese traders arrived in 1485, they encountered a sophisticated court where Oba Ozolua received them with confidence as the representative of a powerful state — not as a lesser power awed by European visitors. Benin exported pepper, ivory, and enslaved people to Europe, while importing brass, cloth, and manufactured goods. The kingdom's brass-casting guilds (the igun-eronmwon) used imported European brass to create the extraordinary portrait plaques and royal heads that constitute the [[event:3062|Benin Bronzes]]. The Oba's control of external trade — including his ultimate authority over access to European traders — was a key source of royal revenue and power. The kingdom declined gradually from the late 17th century onward due to internal succession disputes and the destabilizing effects of the slave trade; British forces destroyed Benin City in the punitive expedition of 1897, seizing the great bronze collection.
